findings.title: libumsg.so: 1 functions call multiple unsafe imports
findings.description: Functions calling 2+ different dangerous C functions are prime targets. With no NX/canary/PIE, any overflow is directly exploitable. Top targets: method.nv.getFreeMemory_unsigned_int_ (each calling ['sscanf', 'fgets'])

findings.title: libumsg.so: 25 exported APIs reach execve()
findings.description: Transitive data flow from exported message/handler APIs to execve: _ZNK2nv7message3hasINS_14addr6, _ZNK2nv7message4sizeINS_7bool_, _ZN2nv17prependLooperFromEPNS_, _ZN2nv9HTTPFetch11onSSLdReplyE, _ZN2nv7message6removeINS_6u32_. If network input flows through these exports, command injection is possible.

findings.title: libumsg.so: 6 end-to-end attack paths to dangerous sinks
findings.description: Mapping: dangerous_function → libumsg_export → network_binary. These paths connect network-facing services to dangerous C functions through the libumsg IPC backbone. Top paths: execve←method.nv.exec_strin←_ZNK2nv7message3hasI←; sprintf←sym.bytes2str_unsign←_ZNK2nv7message3hasI←; sprintf←sym.bytes2str_unsign←_ZNK2nv7message3hasI←; strcpy←method.nv::Logger.co←_ZNK2nv7message3hasI←; strcpy←method.nv::Looper.co←_ZNK2nv7message3hasI←

password_secrets.value: configuration flagged, check all router configuration for unauthorized changes and update device-mode
